item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This was moved from SCP-245 to SCP-2623 with specific explicit permission from the administration given the nature of the project I put into the SCP-245 slot. It should not be taken as a precedent of any kind as to permission to do any such action in the future.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Man, imagine being sent that letter, then realizing that it's not for you but it's for the other Karen chick across the hall.

Then also realizing that you are now anomalous solely because of a mailing/name error. Ah, similar surnames.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I just realised, after rereading, that the letter itself must have imbued its recipient with the teleportation power. That's a great touch. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
small nitpick.
"27 yea old female" and "corpse of a 29 year old male" strikes me as poor tone - it should specify human male/female, as both words don't really include that meaning. I know it's obvious from context, but still.

Otherwise ,yeah, I upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: E: Apologies, I missed the staff post later in the thread. I just saw this and had a knee-jerk reaction.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Up voting for the interesting and less - than - standard teleport mechanics, and the mail mix-up.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Is this really an issue to anyone? Or are we just feeding a troll?

I like idea of a mail screw-up giving the anomaly to the "wrong" person. It's the kind of thing that might make nieve, intuitive sense to a child.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, I don't disagree with this, I just find it amusing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wait, there's enjoyment to be had out of P&P? The only way I could make it (halfway, which was all that was needed for the class) through that book was imagining that this was all happening in the middle of an alien invasion (which happened before I knew of Grahame-Smith's version, FYI).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think that's because you can dismantle a nuclear bomb with no ethical qualms, and because a nuclear bomb can't detonate itself (besides that one bomb), while the exact opposite is true of a human being. Granted, a human "detonating" themselves usually just makes a mess and doesn't kill 100,000 people, but it's easier to SCIENCE! a person than it is to SCIENCE! a corpse.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Replying to self to say that I think -1 should be the lady, and 245 should be the corpse, because the corpse was recovered first and lead to the discovery of the lady.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Upvoted for two things.

The second is the missent mail, which was obviously intended to be the punchline of the article, but it's worth reiterating that it's a damn good twist. Especially since that, given the nature of the relationship as it was presented up to this point, it's entirely plausible that 245 would just flatly deny that she's ever known 245-1.

The first thing, which is something that I really like, is the containment method. Here's this woman who can teleport any time from anywhere and we can't do anything to stop her. So we just go ahead and build a prison around her destination and now she can teleport all she likes.

Also, while I probably agree with the change to the object class, I find it somewhat amusing that the Foundation is classifying what is at this point a mostly ordinary human being as Euclid. I think if they had to lock up some completely average non-anomalous person for some reason, they'd class her/him as Euclid too. A single human is more dangerous than a nuclear bomb as far as the Foundation is concerned.

item.title: Staff Post - Closed
item.content:encoded:
After reading this whole thread, and discussing with some staff members who were un-involved, I'm ordering the specific topic of sexuality and whether or not the couple should be gay or straight or gay alien mushrooms to be dropped.

Cimmerian made a particular choice in his mind about the nature of the relationship portrayed here, and while it is perfectly fine to comment on that fact, the exact nature of discussion in this thread is progressing steadily into a very poor region of critique.

Don't try to enforce your personal preferences as How-Things-Should-Be-Done.

This is an important part of the Criticism Policy. While we usually use this to refer to instances where people are told things like "lolFoundation is always bad" or "No author avatars, ever," a lot of the comments in this thread fall in that category as well. Saying that "Making things gay=more interesting," or "This needs to be gay or else I'll downvote" fall squarely under this guideline. Do not enforce your own personal beliefs when critiquing. People are free to write what they want, and you are free to vote what way you want, but flat-out telling the author that the story is "fucking boring" because it's not gay crosses the line.

I hope there are more constructive areas for criticism aside from the sexuality of the relationship.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Agreed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't think you'd find anyone going into the comments section of a tale about a gay couple to tell the author to "make it straighter", so I don't get why people would do the same thing here.

I agree with you. However, to be fair, I have seen it claimed in a few places (Moose says so below, for example) but to be truthful, I haven't ever seen it happen. That's not to say it didn't happen, tho.

I think we don't need all this drama! :D Love is love, let's just leave it at that and get along. (Hopefully.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Seconding this, the very idea that an article is homophobic for not including a gay couple is one of the most nonsensical things I've ever heard. "Make it gayer" isn't anything close to critique. I don't think you'd find anyone going into the comments section of a tale about a gay couple to tell the author to "make it straighter", so I don't get why people would do the same thing here.

That aside, the article is, I feel, uninteresting for reasons beyond the sexuality of the two main characters. If this were about a gay couple, it would still be boring.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
When I read Cimmerian's first draft, I told him that yes, we've seen this story a thousand times, spurned guy pines for lost female love and tries to trick/influence her into reciprocating. Boring.

But instead of adding gay, I recommended, what if she wrote the note instead of he? We've seen the pressuring guy tons, but we never see the pressuring gal. Cimmerian went with the mixed post, instead, which does add interest to the piece, but I think the "not enough gay" angle is really more of a "this conforms too predictably to gender norms."

Giving the central characters different genders does add to the piece. It reminds us that the stock story of man-chases-woman exists in other situations, too. It doesn't have to be a man chasing a woman. A man chasing a man. A Dom chasing a sub. It exists in all directions, across different orientations, along different axes.

But let's say we want to keep the man-chases-woman angle and play that straight (for whatever reason, not my ideal choice). How would I update it? I would have -1 send the letter to Miss Taylor James. It's just that the post regularly mixes it up with Mr. Taylor Jones.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think I understand what you mean, so I'm gonna try to clarify… I might be very wrong, so feel free to correct me if I interpreted your thing wrong.

I was agreeing with Mac in that having heterosexual characters is not homophobic. Having heterosexual characters because gay characters were avoided because they're gay is homophobic, and I got no vibes like that from this article whatsoever.

Also, this wiki has no chill anymore.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The idea that you should include them because it's more interesting, however, falls into the exact same fallacy MacLeod's falling into.

Agreed. This is actually something that Cimmerian gets, and was discussed elsewhere, but wasn't clear in my argument here. Whoops.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Not allowing people to write, draw, or otherwise create whatever they want is always wrong. Period.

You yourself have tried to get authors to change other things in SCP articles, I assume.

"Downvoting for heterosexuality" is not worse than "Downvoting for author avatars". Even though I don't endorse either one. (And, in fact, I absolutely loathe the latter.)

The rest of this is really answered elsewhere.

For clarity: Cimmerian is only being forced to take this particular critique if we, Foundation staff, force him to take it. I assure you, that is never going to happen under any circumstances.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Downvoting for heterosexual relationships is, I feel, generally a kind of dumb reason to downvote. Generally. There are exceptions, like if there are a lot of different relationships in an article, you'd expect at least some of them to be homosexual, queer, what have you. However, this is not a protected topic where downvotes are concerned. If someone feels they should downvote, then they can downvote, and I'm not gonna tell them they're wrong.

That said, "This could stand a bit more representation" is a valid criticism. There's a very strong tendency among our writers to default to the "norm" when creating characters. They're usually white. They're male a lot more often than female. They're usually straight. In the context of a single article, no, there's no reason to make a given character black, or gay, or queer. But in the context of all the articles on the wiki it becomes more obviously a problem. And saying, "Hey, this would be a good opportunity to have a character who isn't generic straight white dude" is good criticism.

The idea that you should include them because it's more interesting, however, falls into the exact same fallacy MacLeod's falling into. It's the idea that you can only deviate from the norm to score specific points. You can only have gay characters when you want to showcase the gay experience of forbidden love. You can only include black characters when you want to explore racism. This is a shitty line of thinking. It turns them permanently into the Other. You can have gay, queer or minority characters without it being a Thing. This doesn't mean you should never explore those themes, but minorities don't disappear when their unique issues with society aren't being addressed. Black people don't vanish when the topic of racism isn't being addressed. Gays don't stop being gay when there isn't any homophobia going on.

Let me put it like this: If you have to ask why a character needs to be queer, ask yourself this: Why do they need to be cis? Do you need to be addressing the specific topic of heterosexuality to have straight people? Is there some inherent issue facing white people that has to come up before you include them?

You can use minority characters for no other reason than because they exist in real life and are ignored pretty often in TV shows, books and movies. Don't just do it when the story calls for it, don't do it because it's more "interesting," do it because it kind of sucks to read stories and never see anyone like yourself there. Do it because by doing so, you help normalize minorities, so they can stop being the Other and start being Us.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Coolbeans. I'm glad we didn't drag this out for an eternity. You have yourself a good day and I hope next time we chat it's about something funner and more interesting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"Not allowing people to write, draw, or otherwise create whatever they want is always wrong. Period."

I have some very serious reservations and disagreements with this statement but it's all cool.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Firstly, I'm not talking about you specifically or anyone else. I'm talking about the notion itself.

Secondly, which group has or hasn't been oppressed is completely irrelevant. It wouldn't be different at all. The fact that such-and-such group is oppressed, under-represented, or otherwise given the short end of the stick by society does not and will never mean that forcing a standard on a writer isn't wrong. Not allowing people to write, draw, or otherwise create whatever they want is always wrong. Period.

If you want more gay or whatever characters, go create them yourself. Don't expect anyone else to do it for you because that is against freedom of expression.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm not sayin they can't. I'm saying it's horribly boring. And it's actually a lot different than saying making a gay character straight would be better. Historically, straight people have not been oppressed for being heterosexual. Whereas gay people have been oppressed for being gay. It's as ridiculous a notion as the concept of reverse racism.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I didn't say downvoting for heterosexuality infringed on peoples' rights. I said it was against peoples' rights to forward the idea that one sexuality is inherently better or "more interesting" than another.

You're essentially telling people that they can't write a character a certain way, which is wrong. For the same reasons it would be wrong of me to tell you that you need to change this or that gay character because being straight would be better.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Downvoting for heterosexuality is not infringing upon anyone's rights. Downvoting has nothing to do with anyone's "rights."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm with you as well, MacLeod.

I'm shocked that you can get downvoted for writing a heterosexual relationship. Advocating the visibility of less-visible persons is fine but I'll be damned if it comes at the price of infringing on the rights of people to write what they want to write. It's offensive to me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I honestly didn't like it, I generally don't like romance in writing or movie or any form, I find it to be dull and so commonly repeated. The best example of romantic writing that didn't literally kill me of boredom was Pride and Prejudice and that still had me bored until about the last three chapters. Perhaps the excitement of final conclusion had me happier in those chapters.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I actually completely disagree. At this point in my life, seeing another boring heterosexual union played out, especially with the Weird Man who Loves Her SOOO Much, is terribly annoying. It is downvote worthy in my opinion. It's expected and adds nothing of depth to the tale. Homosexuality, however, would offer a lot. Homosexuality is upvote-worthy in and of itself.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My reply still stands with the edit.

I just don't think this is offensive

Neither does anyone else, as I said.

and don't think making it gay would make it more interesting.

I explained this already. But the rest of my analysis will go under a cut.

+ show block
– hide block

Literally any detail that's less expected makes something more interesting. This is commonly done on the character level by adding unusual background details, atypical professions, surprising personality quirks, bodily scars (abused by cinema until a single scar across an eye became just another cliche).

Sexual orientation of characters is not an exception to this rule. When almost every character is X, then making a character Y is more interesting by default.

If it doesn't interest you personally, that's fine, and perfectly reasonable, but I'm talking general principle here, because there's literally zero point trying to debate personal taste in this case.

I also don't think the author should feel inclined to change a work when they did nothing wrong.

That is not how editing works. You don't need to do something wrong to have a reason to change it. In fact, in professional editing, it often amounts to replacing one perfectly fine thing with another perfectly fine thing, just to keep your editor happy, but I don't mean that here.

I mean that if you only improve things by changing the things that are actually wrong, then you won't get very far. The best writers don't settle for merely not wrong.

Look up earlier drafts of masterpiece works sometime to see what I mean. Or even just shooting script vs final script. This can be… illuminating. My favorite, easy-to-find example is the initial pilot of BBC Sherlock, versus the final pilot. Both are very good. The second is drastically better.

EDIT: For clarity, this is about good writing principle. It does not necessarily apply to this particular change to this particular story.

– hide block

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
psst, moose. Did you see the edit I made to my comment, where I say there'd be nothing wrong if it had been gay from the get-go? I just don't think this is offensive, and don't think making it gay would make it more interesting. More relatable to the atypically gendered and sexually-oriented and such, maybe, but not more inherently interesting. I also don't think the author should feel inclined to change a work when they did nothing wrong.

I will admit that extraterrestrial/talking mushroom romance was a bad example, and that it would actually be fantastic to see on this site. Someone go write that. We need fungus X spaceman stories.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I never claimed offense. It's just so fucking boring. Heterosexual romance is sooooo boring.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Writing a heterosexual relationship is probably the most homophobic thing I can possibly imagine. I will wildly point at this statement every day and everyone else should too

EDIT: Some people understood this, some didn't. That's my faut; I should clarify.

There is no serious analysis suggesting that writing a heterosexual relationship is a homophobic act. Wildly agreeing with it pretends it's actually a real thing, and validates it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I wasn't actually going to say anything about this beyond maybe a joke comment (I gave Cimmerian extensive feedback elsewhere, and it wasn't exactly 'make it gayer') but this argument is starkly off-base.

(Although, as an aside, I have to admit I think the comments about making it gayer are incredibly funny to me, given that those comments usually only appear on pieces about queer characters demanding that they be made straighter. Though I know Cimmerian is understandably confused.)

Anyway, response under the cut.

+ show block
– hide block

The general issue (not specific to this article; no raindrop feels responsible for the flood, but still) is that defaulting to straight/cis/white/male/etc people in every story is that it's a cliche, often dull, and often artistically inferior, aside from being socially negative.

Your argument has the implication that you shouldn't include queer or interracial relationships without a special plot reason for it, which is usually how things are, and also why queer and interracial relationships barely exist in media, unlike real life. This is a problem. Limiting our existence to 'issue stories' is a problem.

Also, the story does ignore gay people. Since there's only two people there, and it's irrelevant to the story, it's not necessarily bad, but it is literally true.

Making a character here gay or trans or an extraterrestrial or a talking mushroom doesn't matter, and, even if gay people are underrepresented in media, this story isn't a situation wherein arms should be taken up over it.

Yes it does. Doing any of these things would make the story more interesting, even if by only a little, while also bringing other effects into the story (such as comedy, etc). This is what happens every time you move away from the default, from what the reader is trained to expect.

It's also an especially weird argument because making them talking mushrooms would make the entire piece obviously, drastically, dramatically different, and I imagine if you reflect on that, you'll agree.

(gay……. alien………. mushrooms…………..)

and I think I've taken the needs of the people claiming offense into account.

No one's claimed offense. They've claimed boredom, and were being silly about it in tone.

– hide block

Alright. Now for my own feedback, also under a cut.

+ show block
– hide block

I already gave Cimmerian feedback elsewhere, but here we go: I don't find this very compelling. It's certainly alright, but it reads as boring to me.

A note on the letter, which others will disagree with. The letter wasn't compelling enough to me — I watched a let's play of Silent Hill 2's In Water ending, which had a really heartbreaking letter, and which I couldn't help but compare this unfavorably to. Though it isn't bad, and it's the thing I'd change last if I were trying to improve this.

Yes, making the characters queer would make it more interesting, but not by very much; it wouldn't fix the other issues. I'd upvote if that change were made, but that's for personal reasons (it would add just enough extra resonance). I'm an easy upvote (really). It wouldn't alone actually fix the issues I perceive.

– hide block

tl;dr, I would wager that if this work were sufficiently interesting, no one would think to question the generic heterosexuality.

(even though everything should be gay, PANSEXUAL NONBINARY DREADLORDS FOREVER)

EDIT: Someone asked why I would upvote for the reason of a queer character in this case. I'll copy my [edited] conversation with Cimmerian to explain why.

Personal opinions included. Click with care.

+ show block
– hide block

<thedeadlymoose> But yeah, to give a quicker answer to your earlier question, changing races/genders/etc away from the default is 'better' just in the same sense that changing any detail from a default is 'better'. The reader always wants to see what's unexpected, as a general rule
<thedeadlymoose> That's the only sense it's meant in. (Obviously, 'default' people are not better than 'non-default' people or traits.)
<thedeadlymoose> It wouldn't actually fix the issues I perceive
<Cimmerian> I get what you're saying. It would still have the same problems but it would resonate more with you?
<thedeadlymoose> Yes. I would upvote largely for that reason.
<thedeadlymoose> I'm an easy upvote, frankly.
<thedeadlymoose> But artistically speaking, you ought to do more. And the comments identifying that as the Real Problem are, frankly, vapid. In my unofficial opinion.

– hide block

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thirded

Writing a heterosexual relationship isn't homophobic; bashing gay people or ignoring them is, and this story doesn't do that.

/wildly points to this

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Basically seconding this. I thought it was just a love story gone wrong, nothing more, nothing less; I don't see the homophobia or reason to think it's homophobia at all.

Edit: In accordance woth MacLeod's edit, I am once again seconding.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ok, here's my take on sexual identity and orientation as far as fiction is concerned:

+Show rant 
-Hide Rant 

In a situation where the sexual orientation or identity of the characters provides context that changes if the orientations or identities change, then write what conveys the intention best. An example? The film "Brokeback Mountain." (Note: I've never seen the film, only heard and read plot summaries and the like.) If you switched one of the two characters to a woman and made the relationship cis-het, it literally becomes meaningless. The emotional impact of that film came from the forbidden love angle, which came from two manly men who were also gay and in love with each other. Another, similar example is the interracial relationship in the novel (and film adaptation) "Holes."

Here, the emotional impact comes from a misunderstanding in the delivery of a letter, and a tragedy that follows. Making a character here gay or trans or an extraterrestrial or a talking mushroom doesn't matter, and, even if gay people are underrepresented in media, this story isn't a situation wherein arms should be taken up over it.

Would making this story about gay people add the forbidden love angle, or make the characters more relatable to the gay folks who read this site? Perhaps, but I don't think it's an offense, or really necessary. Writing a heterosexual relationship isn't homophobic; bashing gay people or ignoring them is, and this story doesn't do that.

-Hide Rant 

Tl;dr: I don't think it's being offensive and don't see the source of offense, and I think I've taken the needs of the people claiming offense into account.

Edit: let me elaborate a little bit more. If the author had originally written this as gay, I would be fine with that. There's nothing wrong with gay romantic tragedy wherein the tragedy doesn't come from the sexual orientation of the characters. I just don't think the author should feel a need to change something that isn't actively offensive.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think that it would be just as powerful a story if their relationship were gay or any other dynamic, and thus, the folks' sexual preferences really play no part. The point of it is the connection between two souls, and love is love.

E: Ohhhhhhhh, you're talking about Addendum 3! Durrrr. Yeah, that could totally be elaborated upon.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is just unnecessarily heterosexual, and I cannot abide that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You're the 2nd person to mention that. I think I actually will upgrade it to Euclid.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was gonna complain about the letter being too "explains the skip", but the third addendum really blows that out of the water. That said, Safe? She's human. They've nullified her anomalous power by containing her at that site, but she can still do stuff.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the connection being the mail being delivered incorrectly but I feel like you could've done a lot more with this and instead did just enough. As a recovering just-enougher, I can't really upvote something like that. It's not bad though. 0 vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I do like how the dead scip and the live scip have no actual connection beyond the mail screw ups. That said, it feels too much like a cheap feels-shot to really do much for me. Still, it's not bad, so novote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: An exceptional and quaint little article article that brings out the feels. Nice one, man.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This isn't even a little gay when it totally could be. I can't really get into it. -1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
AAUUUGGGHHHHHH THAT SONG

I have written two SCPs listening to that song

take your upvote and my tears

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is depressing. Will giving you upvotes make it go away?

No? Well here, I'm gonna try anyway.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ show block
– hide block

Going back to the corner where I first saw you… gonna camp in my sleeping bag I'm not gonna move…

Thanks to Zolgamax, WrongJohnSilver, EldritchCyanide, Decibelles, and TwistedGears for looking at this in chat.
